intro.kicker: Topic 4 of 38 • Chapter: Friction • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: The common misconception that friction always opposes motion is a direct NEET trap. Friction can equally be the cause of forward motion: a walking person, a cycling rear wheel, and a body in an accelerating vehicle all move forward because of friction, not despite it. The critical condition for a body to stay at rest in an accelerating vehicle is ma < μmg, i.e., the acceleration must not exceed μg. NEET tests both the conceptual identification (which body has friction acting forward?) and the inequality condition.

pills.text: ma < μmg for body to stay

expectedQuestions.description: 1 conceptual or scenario MCQ per paper. NEET presents diagrams of a person walking, a bicycle, or a body on an accelerating cart and asks: ‘in which direction does friction act on the rear wheel?’ or ‘what is the condition for the body not to slip?’ Both types are from this topic.

timeRequired.description: 45 min: 20 min concept + 25 min MCQ on walking directions, bicycle, and accelerating vehicle. Draw free-body diagrams for all three scenarios below before attempting questions.

difficulty.description: Moderately difficult due to direction ambiguity. Students must correctly identify whether friction on each part is forward or backward, which requires careful FBD analysis. The bicycle scenario (front vs rear wheel directions differ) is the most common exam trap in this topic.

nriUsaGap.description: AP Physics covers traction and friction roles but does not explicitly contrast front wheel vs rear wheel directions. The NEET formulation of ‘friction as cause of motion’ with the specific vehicle acceleration condition (ma < μmg) is a Class 11 NCERT focus not stressed in AP Physics.

weightage.sectionTitle: Friction as Cause of Motion — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Particularly high-value conceptual topic: friction direction questions appear in most papers.
The bicycle front vs rear wheel question is a recurring format.
Condition ma < μmg appears both as a formula derivation and as a numerical MCQ.

strategySteps.title: Draw FBDs for All Three Scenarios
strategySteps.description: Person walking: friction on foot is forward (ground pushes walker forward). Pedalling bicycle: rear wheel friction is forward; front wheel friction is backward. Accelerating vehicle: friction on loose body is forward (cause of its acceleration) provided ma < μmg.

strategySteps.title: Apply the Condition ma < μmg
strategySteps.description: A body (mass m) rests on an accelerating vehicle (acceleration a). It will move with the vehicle, i.e., not slip, provided the required force ma is less than the maximum static friction μmg. If a > μg, the body slips backward relative to the vehicle.

strategySteps.title: Identify the Primary Conclusion
strategySteps.description: The textbook conclusion: ‘without friction, motion cannot be started, stopped or transferred from one body to another.’ This is a direct NEET statement MCQ answer. Friction enables both the start (walking) and transfer (belt drive, bicycle) of motion.

cards.title: Friction as Cause of Motion Notes
cards.description: All three scenarios (walking, bicycle, accelerating vehicle) with FBD diagrams, condition derivation, and 8 solved NEET-pattern MCQs.

cards.title: Friction Direction Summary
cards.description: One-page reference: friction direction for walker, cyclist (front and rear wheels), and body on accelerating vehicle, with condition table.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Friction as Cause of Motion

cards.title: Friction Enables Walking and Driving
cards.coreRevision: Walking: foot pushes ground backward (action); friction pushes foot forward (reaction). Vehicle rear wheel: wheel pushes ground backward; friction acts forward (propels vehicle). Front coasting wheel: friction acts backward (resists rolling). This is why a pedalled bicycle’s rear and front wheels have opposite friction directions.
points: Action-reaction pair: foot/wheel pushes ground; ground’s friction pushes back on foot/wheel
Rear wheel (driven): friction acts forward = cause of motion
Front wheel (coasting): friction acts backward = opposes rolling
If pedalling stopped: both wheels coast, both experience backward friction
Without friction, a vehicle’s wheels would spin in place (‘wheelspin’)
cards.example: In a car on wet ice (μs ≈ 0): driven wheels spin, car does not move. Add sand (increased μ) and traction is restored — friction is literally the cause of the vehicle’s acceleration.

cards.title: Body on Accelerating Vehicle
cards.coreRevision: A mass m on a vehicle accelerating at a: friction on m = ma (forward). Condition to not slip: ma ≤ μmg, i.e., a ≤ μg. If a > μg the body slides backward relative to vehicle.
points: Only friction provides the forward force to accelerate the body along with the vehicle
Maximum friction available = μmg (vertical N = mg on horizontal surface)
No-slip condition: ma ≤ μmg ⇒ a ≤ μg
If a > μg: body slides backward (friction becomes kinetic, still forward but insufficient)
NEET question type: find maximum vehicle acceleration for an object not to slip
cards.example: Block on truck floor, μs = 0.3, g = 10. Maximum safe acceleration = 0.3 × 10 = 3 m/s². If truck accelerates at 5 m/s², the block slips backward relative to the truck.

curriculum.hintText: AP Physics vs NEET on friction as cause of motion.

gaps.title: Bicycle Wheel Directionality
gaps.description: AP Physics covers traction in general but does not present the NEET-specific format of contrasting front vs rear wheel friction directions during pedalling vs coasting.
bullets: Pedalling: rear wheel friction = forward, front wheel friction = backward.
Coasting (no pedalling): both wheels have backward friction.
This is a favourite NEET FBD question — draw it once before the exam.

gaps.title: Explicit No-Slip Condition
gaps.description: AP Physics uses F = ma and frictional constraints, but NEET explicitly states the no-slip condition as ma < μmg and uses it as a direct MCQ formula. Memorise this inequality.
bullets: a_max = μg is the maximum vehicle acceleration for the block not to slide.
NEET MCQ: ‘A block rests on a truck with μ = 0.5. The maximum retardation for the block not to slide is —’ Answer: 5 m/s².

questions.question: When a person is walking on a rough road, the force of friction on the person acts:

options.text: Backward, in the direction opposite to motion
Forward, in the direction of motion
Perpendicular to the direction of motion
Zero, since the person is not sliding

questions.explanation: When walking, the foot pushes the ground backward (action). By Newton’s third law, the ground exerts an equal and opposite friction force on the foot in the forward direction. This forward friction is the cause of the person’s forward motion.

questions.question: A box of mass m is placed on the floor of a truck. If the truck accelerates at a, the friction on the box acts forward and the box will not slide if:
questions.explanation: The only horizontal force on the box is static friction (forward). For no slipping: ma ≤ μmg (maximum static friction), which simplifies to a ≤ μg. If a > μg, the required friction exceeds the maximum available, so the box slides backward.

questions.question: A vehicle accelerates forward. In which direction does friction act on the driven rear wheel from the ground?
A block of mass 2 kg rests on the floor of a truck. The coefficient of static friction is 0.4 (g = 10 m/s²). The maximum acceleration the truck can have without the block sliding is:
Without friction, which of the following would be impossible?
options: Flow of current in a wire

questions.question: When a bicycle is pedalled forward, the friction on the front (coasting) wheel acts:

items.title: Draw friction FBD for a walking person
items.description: Foot pushes ground backward; friction on foot acts forward. Net force = forward friction = cause of acceleration. Direction must be correct in FBD.

items.title: Explain friection direction for rear and front bicycle wheels
items.description: Rear wheel (driven): ground friction is forward. Front wheel (coasting): ground friction is backward. Coasting both wheels: both backward friction.

items.title: Apply no-slip condition ma ≤ μmg
items.description: A body on an accelerating vehicle will not slip if a ≤ μg. Maximum safe vehicle acceleration = μg. This inequality is the main numerical output of this topic.

items.title: State NCERT conclusion verbatim
items.description: Without friction, motion cannot be started, stopped, or transferred from one body to another. This is a direct NEET statement MCQ answer.

items.question: How can friction be the cause of motion if it always opposes relative motion?
items.answer: Friction opposes relative motion BETWEEN the two surfaces in contact, not the absolute motion of the object. When your foot pushes the ground backward, the ground has a tendency to move backward relative to the foot. Friction opposes this tendency, so it acts forward on the foot. The foot’s absolute motion is forward — caused by friction. There is no contradiction.

items.question: What happens when a car accelerates on ice?
items.answer: On ice, μ ≈ 0. The driven wheels can produce almost no traction force (friction ≈ 0), so the net horizontal force on the car body from the ground is near zero. The wheels spin but the car does not accelerate. This directly confirms that friction is the cause of vehicle acceleration — remove it and motion stops.

items.question: If a box on a truck does not slide, what is the friction force on the box?
items.answer: If the truck accelerates at a m/s² and the box (mass m) does not slide, then the box also accelerates at a. The net force on the box = ma. The only horizontal force is static friction, so friction = ma (forward). This is less than μmg, so no sliding occurs.

items.question: In a bicycle, why do front and rear wheels experience friction in opposite directions?
items.answer: The rear wheel is driven by the pedal mechanism: it pushes ground backward, so friction on it is forward (driving force). The front wheel is not powered: it rolls freely and has a tendency to decelerate, so friction acts backward on it, opposing its rolling. If pedalling stops, both wheels coast and both experience backward friction from the ground.

grounding_summary: Page 237 explicitly states: ‘It is a general misconception that friction always opposes the motion.’ and provides three examples: walking (forward friction), cycling (rear wheel forward, front backward), and body on accelerating vehicle (ma < μmg).
The page gives the key condition for body staying in vehicle: body will remain at rest in the accelerating vehicle until ma < μmg, directly underpinning the numerical MCQ format.
The NCERT conclusion on page 237 is: without friction, motion cannot be started, stopped or transferred from one body to another.
grounding_excerpt: It is a general misconception that friction always opposes the motion.
While moving, a person or vehicle pushes the ground backwards (action) and the rough surface of ground reacts and exerts a forward force due to friction which causes the motion.
From these examples it is clear that without friction, motion cannot be started, stopped or transferred from one body to the other.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part I — Laws of Motion (Friction as cause of motion)
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source for friction direction analysis during walking, cycling, and vehicle acceleration as tested in NEET.

sources.title: NEET Physics Friction MCQs Set A with Answers
sources.why_used: Confirms NEET MCQ patterns for friction-as-cause-of-motion direction questions and the vehicle acceleration condition.

review.notes: Content grounded in page-237 OCR markdown. All three scenarios from NCERT covered. Condition ma < μmg correctly derived. No dual_cta.
